cues.x: Can't Hurt Me, by David Goggins.
Welcome back to Listen Up.
Settle in, because today we are spending about fifty minutes with one of the most intense human beings who has ever put his life on paper.
The book is called Can't Hurt Me, and its subtitle tells you exactly what David Goggins is after: Master Your Mind and Defy the Odds.
This is not a gentle book.
It is not going to pat you on the head and tell you that you are already enough.
It is going to grab you by the collar and ask you a question you have probably been avoiding your whole life: how much of yourself have you actually used?
That is the frame for everything that follows.
Goggins believes that most of us live inside a tiny fraction of our real capacity.
We think we are giving a hundred percent when we are giving forty.
We stop when it gets uncomfortable and we call that our limit, when it is really just the first door in a very long hallway.
The whole book is built to prove that idea, and it proves it in an unusual way.
It alternates.
One chapter is his life story, raw and often hard to hear.
The next is a challenge, a specific assignment he hands directly to you, the reader, so that the lessons do not stay theoretical.
He does not want you to admire him.
He wants you to go do something hard today.
So as we walk through his life, keep one ear open for yourself.
Somewhere in this story is a mirror.
And I want to be honest with you up front about the tone of this one.
There are episodes where I get to tell you a charming, cozy story.
This is not one of them.

This is a story about a person who was handed just about the worst possible starting hand, who spent the first two decades of his life losing over and over, and who then did something almost no one does.
He did not wait for the world to get fairer.
He did not wait to feel ready.

He decided that the only variable he could truly control was his own effort, and then he pushed that one variable so far past what looked reasonable that he rewrote what a human being appears to be capable of.
Whether or not you ever run a single mile, that decision, that shift from waiting to acting, is the thing worth stealing from this book.
Let us start at the beginning, in Buffalo, New York.
From the outside, young David had something that looked like the good life.
His father, Trunnis, owned a roller skating rink called Skateland.
Neon lights, music, wheels on a wooden floor, kids laughing.
To the neighborhood it looked like the family was doing well.
But behind the neon was a home built on fear.
The rink did not run on joy.
It ran on the unpaid labor of Trunnis's own children, who worked deep into the night, hour after hour, when they should have been sleeping or being kids.
And when the work was not fast enough, or when the mood turned, there was violence.
Real violence, aimed at the children and at their mother, Jackie.
Goggins does not tell this part for shock.
He tells it because it shaped the machinery of his mind.
A child who grows up bracing for the next blow learns to live in a state of constant alertness.
He learns that the world is not safe and that no one is coming.
That wiring, which was carved into him by terror, is the same wiring he would later aim, on purpose, at goals most people would call impossible.
But that comes later.
First there was just a little boy, exhausted, frightened, watching his mother absorb pain and trying to make himself invisible.
It is worth pausing here on something the book handles with real care.
Goggins never presents his childhood as a gift.
He is not one of those people who says the abuse made him who he is and grins about it.
The abuse was a crime committed against a child, full stop.
What he claims is something subtler and more useful: that later in life he learned to reach back into that early darkness and use the survival instincts it forged as fuel.
The pain was not good.

But the pain was not going away either, and at some point he made a decision that the pain could either drive him into the ground or drive him forward, and that the direction was, at least partly, up to him.
That is a distinction worth holding onto, because it is the difference between glorifying suffering and refusing to let suffering have the last word.
Eventually Jackie did the bravest thing in the whole story.
She got out.
She took David and his brother and she left Trunnis and the neon behind, and she ran to Brazil, Indiana.
That escape cost her everything financially.
They went from the appearance of comfort to genuine poverty, from the skating rink to scraping by.
But she chose poverty and safety over comfort and terror, and Goggins never forgets what that choice cost her or what it gave him: a chance.
Brazil, Indiana, though, brought a new kind of danger.
They were one of the very few Black families in a small, mostly white town, and the racism there was not subtle or polite.
It was notes left for them.
It was slurs.
It was the feeling, which no child should ever carry, of being hunted in the place that was supposed to be home.
And it got worse than words.
A man who was close to his mother was murdered, and the violence of the outside world fused with the memory of the violence inside the old home.
Think about what that does to a young mind.
The place he escaped to was not safe either.
There was no sanctuary, no location on earth where he could fully put the fear down.
The terror was not a thing that happened to him once.
It was the air.
School added a third layer of struggle.
Goggins had learning difficulties that went undiagnosed for years.
He could barely read, and he was terrified of anyone finding out.
So he did what a frightened, ashamed kid does: he faked it.
He cheated.
He copied.
He developed an entire covert operation just to hide the fact that the words on the page would not hold still for him.
And underneath the cheating was a shame so heavy it colored everything.
He believed he was stupid.
He believed he was less than.
When you carry a secret like that, every classroom is a minefield and every raised hand from a teacher is a threat.
That shame followed him toward adulthood.
When he set his sights on the Air Force, he ran into a wall he could not cheat past cleanly: the military entrance exam, the ASVAB.
He failed it.
Then he failed it again.
Two times, a young man who had spent years hiding that he could not read was told, in the plainest institutional language, that he did not measure up.
And here is the first real hinge of the book, the first moment you see the engine that would define his life.
Instead of accepting that verdict, he locked himself away.
He took the thing he was most ashamed of, the academic weakness, and he simply refused to leave it alone.
He ground out the math.
Hour after hour, day after day, attacking the exact subject that had beaten him, until he passed.
He did not become a genius overnight.
He just outlasted his own weakness through sheer, ugly repetition.
Remember that word, repetition.
It is the secret ingredient in almost everything he ever accomplished.
I want you to notice the shape of that story, because it repeats across his whole life like a fingerprint.
Something beats him.
He feels the shame of being beaten.

And then, instead of walking away and finding an easier road, he turns around and marches directly back at the exact thing that defeated him, and he stays there, grinding, long past the point where a reasonable person would give up and choose a different door.
Most of us, when we fail at something, quietly rearrange our lives so we never have to face that thing again.
We tell ourselves we were never really interested in it anyway.
Goggins does the opposite.
He treats the point of failure as a set of coordinates, a place to return to, and he does not consider the matter closed until he has stood on that spot and won.
That pattern is not glamorous while it is happening.
It is lonely, repetitive, and boring.
But it is the whole machine.
So he got into the Air Force and entered the pararescue pipeline, the training to become a PJ, one of the elite rescue specialists.
And then it fell apart.
He was diagnosed with the sickle cell trait, which became the official reason he left the program.
But Goggins, in the honest way that makes this book what it is, admits the fuller truth.
It was not only the diagnosis.
It was fear.
Fear of the water, specifically.
The pool work, the drown-proofing, the being held under, terrified him, and when the medical off-ramp appeared, he took it, and part of him knew he was taking it partly to escape the water.
He quit.
And he would spend a long time paying for that quitting, not with punishment from anyone else, but with the private knowledge that he had folded.
What came next were the lost years, and this is the part of the story that anyone who has ever felt stuck should hold onto.
He became an exterminator.
He would get up in the dark and drive to restaurants and buildings and spray for cockroaches at four in the morning.
He ballooned to close to three hundred pounds.
He would come home and drink chocolate milkshakes late at night, medicating a hollow feeling that no amount of sugar could fill.
He describes looking at himself and seeing a nobody.
Not in a dramatic, self-pitying way, but as a flat fact.
He had a job, he had a routine, and he had a growing certainty that his life was going to be small and disappointing and that it was his own fault.
If you have ever sat in a car at the end of a day and felt the weight of a life that is not the one you meant to build, you already understand the man he was at that moment.
And here is why that stretch matters so much to the story.
It would be easy to hear about the SEAL and the ultramarathons and assume this was always a special person, someone built different from birth, someone for whom the extraordinary was just natural.
The exterminator years demolish that excuse.
Before he was any of the things the world now knows him as, he was overweight, defeated, and going nowhere, doing a job he did not love in the dark, numbing himself with sugar at night.
He was, in other words, a lot like anyone who has ever felt that their best days were probably behind them and their real self had been quietly filed away.
The reason Goggins insists on telling this part in full is that he wants you to know the transformation did not begin from a place of strength.
It began from the bottom.
Which means the bottom is not a disqualification.
The bottom is just where the story happened to start.
Now comes the turning point, and it is almost absurdly ordinary in how it started.
One evening, tired from the roach route, he turned on the television and saw a documentary about Navy SEAL training.
Hell Week.
Men in the surf, in the cold, in the sand, being pushed past what looks humanly bearable.
And something in him, buried under the milkshakes and the shame and the three hundred pounds, sat up.
That, he thought.
That is the hardest thing there is, and I want it.
Not because it looked fun.
Because it looked like the exact opposite of the soft, quitting version of himself he had become.
He wanted to walk straight into the fire he had spent his life avoiding.
So he started calling recruiters.
And he got told no, over and over.
He was too heavy.
He was too old for the easiest path in.
His background did not fit.
Recruiter after recruiter brushed him off.
Finally one of them leveled with him.
To even qualify to try for the SEALs, he would have to lose a staggering amount of weight, more than a hundred pounds, in under three months.
To most people that sentence is a closed door.
To Goggins it was, at last, a target.
A specific, brutal, measurable thing to attack, exactly like the math he had once ground out in that locked room.
What followed was one of the most punishing training blocks you will ever hear described.
He gave himself over to it completely.
Hours a day on the exercise bike.
Long stretches on the treadmill.
Time in the pool, forcing himself back toward the water he feared.
Thousands upon thousands of repetitions, the body screaming, the scale slowly, slowly moving.
He treated his own transformation like a military campaign against his old self.
And he made it.
He dropped the weight, an amount that sounds medically implausible in that window, through nothing more exotic than relentless daily effort.
The nobody spraying for roaches had reengineered his own body to earn a shot at the hardest school in the military.
Then came BUD/S, the SEAL selection course, and here Goggins earns a place in a category almost no one occupies.
BUD/S is designed to break people, and its centerpiece is Hell Week, roughly five and a half days of continuous cold, wet, sleepless misery meant to make you quit.
Most men quit.
Goggins did not quit.
But the universe was not going to hand him a clean pass either.
The first time, he got pneumonia, his lungs filling up while he kept going, until his body could not continue and he had to be rolled back.
The second time, he went through with shattered kneecaps, broken, doing the impossible on legs that were failing, and again he was rolled.
So he did Hell Week not once, not twice, but three separate times.
Read that again and let it sit.
He voluntarily walked back into the single hardest week the military can offer, twice more, after his body had already been wrecked by it.
He finally graduated with Class 235.
Think for a moment about the decision each of those repeats required.
The first Hell Week is a leap into the unknown, and you can do that on adrenaline and ignorance.
But the second and third times, he knew exactly what was waiting.
He knew the cold that never lets up, the sand in every raw patch of skin, the hallucinations that come with days of no sleep, the specific way his body would beg him to quit.
He had already been broken by this exact experience, and he chose it again anyway, eyes open.
That is a completely different kind of courage from the first-timer's.
It is the courage of a man who has already met the monster, been beaten by it, and decided the beating did not settle anything.
Whenever you hear people talk about grit, this is the picture to keep in mind.
Not doing the hard thing once when you did not know better, but going back to it after it has already put you on the ground.
And he did not stop at the SEAL trident.
Over the years that followed, Goggins did something that, as far as anyone can tell, no other human being has done.
He completed Navy SEAL training.
He completed Army Ranger School, and not just completed it, he graduated as the enlisted honor man, the top enlisted soldier in his class.
And he completed Air Force tactical air controller training.
Three of the most demanding military schools in existence, in three different branches, one man.
He turned himself into a kind of walking proof that the ceiling we imagine for ourselves is almost always painted on far too low.
But military schools were not the end of his hunger.
The next chapter of his life turned toward something even stranger: ultra endurance running, and it began in grief.
In 2005, SEAL teammates and friends were killed in Afghanistan.

Goggins wanted to do something that mattered for the children they left behind, so he set out to raise money for the Special Operations Warrior Foundation, which supports the families and children of fallen special operators.
His method was pure Goggins.
He decided to enter a twenty-four hour ultramarathon, a race where you run as far as you can in a full day, and he entered it with essentially no distance training.
He was strong, but he was not a trained ultrarunner.
He just decided that the suffering was the point and the cause was worth it.
What his body went through in that race is hard to hear.
He ran roughly one hundred miles in about a day.
Along the way he broke bones in his feet.
His kidneys began to fail under the strain, and he was passing blood, his body sending every possible signal that it was in serious danger.
And he did not stop.
He kept moving on broken feet with a body in medical crisis, because he had decided in advance that quitting was not on the menu.
Now, hold that image, because later in this episode we are going to talk honestly about the cost of moments exactly like this one.

But in terms of what it revealed about the mind, it revealed that the place where his body said finished and the place where he actually collapsed were separated by an enormous, almost frightening distance.
That first ultra opened a door, and he walked through it into some of the hardest races on the planet.
The most famous is Badwater 135.
One hundred thirty-five miles on foot across Death Valley in the dead of summer, through heat that can melt the soles of your shoes to the road, one of the most brutal footraces that exists.
He ran it.

A man who had recently been three hundred pounds and spraying for cockroaches was now grinding across the desert floor in temperatures that send ordinary people to the hospital, and he kept coming back for more of it.
What is striking, if you step back, is that Goggins entered the endurance world as a total outsider and stayed one for a long time.
He did not come up through running clubs or have the lean, birdlike build of a career ultrarunner.
He was a heavily muscled former SEAL who, by the sport's own logic, was carrying too much weight to be doing this at all.
And yet he showed up at these races and finished them, and sometimes finished near the front, through a willingness to suffer that even seasoned endurance athletes found unusual.
There is a lesson buried in that, and it is not that natural talent does not exist.
It clearly does.

The lesson is that in a great many arenas, the number of people willing to endure real, sustained discomfort is so small that a person who is genuinely willing to suffer can go much further than their raw gifts would predict.
Talent sets a ceiling, yes.
But most people quit so far below their own ceiling that the ceiling almost never becomes the deciding factor.
Willingness does.
Then there is the pull-up record, and this one is important because it is a story about public failure, which most success stories quietly skip.

Goggins decided to go after the Guinness World Record for the most pull-ups in twenty-four hours, and he did it on national television, in front of cameras and an audience, with all the exposure that entails.
The first attempt fell apart.
His body gave out, his hands tore open, and he had to stop, humiliatingly, in public.
So he tried again.
And the second attempt also failed.
Twice, in front of the country, this man who had become a symbol of unbreakable will was visibly, physically broken, unable to finish the thing he had promised to do.
Most of us would never try a third time.
The shame alone would bury us.
He came back a third time and did more than four thousand pull-ups in about seventeen hours, hauling his own bodyweight up to the bar over and over until the number was reached.
The lesson he draws is not glory.
It is that failure in front of everyone did not kill him, and that the third attempt was built directly on the wreckage of the first two.
And then, years into all of this superhuman output, comes the twist that reframes the entire book.
Doctors examining his heart found a hole in it.
An atrial septal defect, a congenital opening between the chambers that should not be there.
Which means that for much of his life, through the ultras and the schools and the record, he had been performing with a heart running well below its proper capacity.
Sit with the implication.
He did not accomplish these things because his body was a perfect machine.
He accomplished them with a defective one, with a heart quietly working at a deficit the whole time.
He underwent open heart surgery to repair it.
By his own accounting his body is a catalog of surgeries and injuries, a ledger of everything the pursuit has taken.
We will return to that ledger honestly before we finish, because Goggins himself insists that you not romanticize it.
Now let us slow down and get to the real value of this book for you, the listener, because Can't Hurt Me is not just a highlight reel of one extraordinary and painful life.
It is a toolbox.
Goggins gives names to specific mental tools, and each one is something you can actually pick up and use in an ordinary life that involves no ultramarathons at all.
Let us walk through them one at a time, with a worked example for each.
The first is the Accountability Mirror.
This is the practice he used to start climbing out of the roach-spraying years.
You stand in front of your bathroom mirror and you tell yourself the brutal truth.
Not the flattering story, not the excuses, the truth.
And then you write the truth, and the specific next goal, on sticky notes and you put them on the mirror where you have to see them every single day.
If you are overweight and unhappy, the note does not say you are beautiful just as you are.
It says the honest thing about where you are and the specific number or action that is next.
Then you face it, every morning, until the note is no longer true, at which point you write a new one.
Here is how you use it.
Pick one area of your life where you have been telling yourself a comfortable lie.
Write the real situation on a note in plain language.
Write the single next concrete action under it.
Put it where you brush your teeth.
The point is not motivation.
The point is that you can no longer pretend you did not see it.
The second tool is what he calls callousing the mind.
Think about the hands of someone who works hard with them.
The skin does not stay soft.
It builds calluses, layers of toughness, precisely in the spots that get the most friction.
Goggins argues the mind works the same way.
You do not toughen your mind by reading about toughness or by feeling inspired.
You toughen it the only way calluses form: by repeated contact with the exact thing that hurts.
Every time you do something uncomfortable on purpose and survive it, you lay down another layer.
The cold shower, the workout you did not feel like doing, the hard conversation you had rather than avoided.
None of them are impressive on their own.
But stacked up over months, they build a mind that does not flinch as easily, because it has been rubbed against difficulty so many times that difficulty has stopped being novel.
To use it, stop outsourcing your discomfort.
Seek out one small, voluntary hard thing each day, and understand that the discomfort is not the price of the growth.
The discomfort is the growth.
There is a flip side to this that is worth naming, because it is where a lot of modern life quietly works against you.
If contact with discomfort builds calluses, then a life engineered for maximum comfort does the opposite.
It sands the calluses off.

Every convenience that removes a small friction from your day, the food that arrives without effort, the entertainment that fills every idle second, the option to avoid anyone who makes you uncomfortable, is also, in a small way, removing a chance to toughen.

Goggins is not against comfort exactly, but he is deeply suspicious of a life with no voluntary hardship in it at all, because he believes such a life leaves you soft in a way you will not notice until the day something hard arrives uninvited.
The practical takeaway is not to make yourself miserable.

It is to make sure that somewhere in your week you are choosing, on purpose, to do a thing that is uncomfortable, so that your capacity for difficulty does not quietly waste away while you are not looking.
The third tool is the most famous idea in the book, the forty percent rule.

Goggins says that when your mind tells you that you are done, that you have hit your absolute limit and cannot take another step, you are actually only somewhere around forty percent of the way to your true capacity.
Not because your mind is lying to hurt you, but because your mind has a governor.
On an engine, a governor is a device that caps the speed, that keeps the machine from redlining and destroying itself.
Your brain has one too, and it engages early, on purpose, to protect you from harm.
That is a good design for survival.
But it means the wall you hit is almost never your real limit.
It is a safety cushion that engages long, long before the actual edge.
Goggins is not telling you the governor is your enemy.

He is telling you it is set far too conservatively for the life most of us want to live, and that with training you can learn to push past that first false ceiling into the reserve that was there the whole time.
To use this one, you do not need a hundred-mile race.

The next time you are certain you are finished, whether it is the last few reps, the last hard hour of work, the moment you want to close the laptop, treat that feeling not as a stop sign but as a marker that says you have more left.
Take a few more steps past it, deliberately, and notice that the world does not end.
That is you meeting your governor and learning it is negotiable.
I want to be careful and fair here, because the forty percent figure is not a measured scientific constant, and Goggins does not really present it as one.

It is a rough, memorable way of pointing at something that is genuinely true: the gap between where your mind first says stop and where your body actually fails is much larger than almost anyone believes until they have deliberately explored it.
Do not get hung up on the exact number.
The useful part is the reframe.

When you learn to treat that first wave of I cannot do this not as a verdict but as an early warning that fires with a huge margin of safety built in, you change your relationship with your own limits forever.
You stop taking the first surge of quit at face value.
You start getting curious about what is actually on the other side of it.
And most of the time, what is on the other side of it is not collapse.
It is simply more road, road you would never have seen if you had turned back at the sign.
The fourth tool is the Cookie Jar, and it is the tender counterpart to all the harshness.
Goggins keeps a mental jar, and into it he has placed every victory, every hardship survived, every time he did something that once seemed impossible.
The pneumonia Hell Week.
The two hundred pounds ground off.
The math conquered in a locked room.
When he is deep in suffering and part of him wants to quit, he reaches into that jar and pulls out a memory of a time he already proved he could keep going.
It is evidence.
It is proof, in his own handwriting, that the person who wants to stop right now has been in worse spots and did not stop then.
To use it, sit down when you are calm and actually make your jar.
Write down the hard things you have already survived, the ones you tend to forget when you are struggling.
The illness you got through.
The loss you endured.
The thing you were sure would break you and did not.
Then, in your next hard moment, reach in.
The point is that motivation runs out but evidence does not, and you have more evidence than you give yourself credit for.
The fifth tool has an aggressive name: Taking Souls.
This is what Goggins does with whoever, or whatever, is trying to break him.
When an instructor or a competitor or a circumstance is convinced it can make him quit, he flips a switch.
He does the very thing they were sure would end him, and he does it with a look on his face that says this is nothing, give me more.
And he watches their certainty collapse.
Taking a soul is the moment the other person realizes their worst has not worked and they have nothing left to threaten you with.
Now, most of your life does not contain a drill instructor.
So how do you use this?
The person trying to break you is often a situation, or a doubt, or an old story about yourself.
Taking a soul can mean showing up cheerful and unbroken at the exact moment a hard circumstance expected to see you crumble.
It steals the power right back.
The suffering does not get a reaction out of you, and something inside you shifts from victim to owner.
The sixth idea he sums up in a phrase: be uncommon amongst the uncommon.
It is easy to think that once you are in a strong group, the group's standard is the standard.
You are in shape compared to the couch, so you relax.
You are hardworking compared to your coworkers, so you coast.
Goggins refuses that math.
The room's standard is not your standard.
Even among people who are already exceptional, he wants to be the one who does more, who stays later, who takes it further.
Not to beat them.
To keep himself honest, because comparing yourself to the people around you is a comfortable way to stop growing.
To use it, notice where you have started grading yourself on the curve of the people nearest you, and quietly set your own bar somewhere above the room.
The seventh tool is visualization, but done in a way that is the opposite of the soft version you usually hear.
Most visualization advice tells you to picture the trophy, the finish line, the applause, the medal around your neck.
Goggins says that is a trap, because it prepares you for the reward and leaves you defenseless against the difficulty.
Instead, he rehearses the suffering.
Before a hard event he plays the pain forward in his mind.
He imagines the exact moments where it will hurt, where he will want to quit, where his body will beg him to stop, and he decides in advance how he will respond to each of them.
So that when the real suffering arrives, it is not a surprise.
It is an old rehearsal he has already run a hundred times.
To use it, before anything you are dreading, do not fantasize about how great it will feel when it is over.
Sit down and honestly picture the hardest parts, and plan your response to them now, while you are calm, so that your plan is already made when the hard part comes.
The eighth idea is a hard truth about motivation, and it is one worth tattooing somewhere.
Motivation is unreliable.
It is a feeling, and feelings come and go with the weather and your sleep and your mood.
If you build your life on motivation, you will do the work on the days you happen to feel like it and skip it on the days you do not, which are most days.
What actually shows up on a cold, dark morning when everything in you wants to stay in bed is not motivation.
It is discipline.
Discipline is a decision you already made, a standard you hold regardless of how you feel.
Goggins does not wait to feel inspired.
He does the thing because it is the thing he does, feeling or no feeling.
To use it, stop asking yourself whether you feel like it.
Decide the standard in advance, and then let the standard, not your mood, make the call.
This is probably the single most practical idea in the book, so let me press on it a little.
Most people treat motivation as the fuel and assume that if they could just feel more of it, the work would take care of itself.
So they hunt for motivation.
They watch the videos, they read the quotes, they wait for the surge of energy that will finally make them get up.
And the surge does come, sometimes, and for a day or two things move.
Then the feeling fades, as feelings always do, and they are back where they started, waiting for the next hit.
Goggins is telling you that this whole cycle is a trap, because it makes your progress hostage to a mood you cannot control.
Discipline breaks the cycle by removing the mood from the equation entirely.
You do not negotiate with yourself each morning about whether you feel like it.
You already decided.
The decision was made once, in advance, when you were clear-headed, and now it simply executes, the way a schedule executes, whether or not you are in the mood.
It is far less exciting than motivation.
It is also the only thing that actually works over the long run.
And the ninth, which ties them all together: no one is coming to save you.
Goggins is blunt that the cavalry is not on its way.
There is no rescue, no lucky break, no person who is going to arrive and do the hard part for you.
And the flip side of that lonely truth is oddly empowering.
If no one is coming to save you, then the only opponent who actually matters is the voice in your own head, the one you are arguing with when you want to stop.
Not your competitors.
Not your circumstances.
That inner voice negotiating for the couch, for the excuse, for the easier road.
That is the fight.
Win the argument with that voice and you win almost everything.
Lose it, and no amount of talent or luck will matter.

Now, I promised you honesty, and this is where a responsible summary has to slow down and be careful, because Can't Hurt Me is a genuinely dangerous book if you take it as a literal instruction manual.
So let us talk squarely about the cost.
Goggins did much of what we described with a body that paid enormous prices.
Broken bones in his feet that he ran on rather than resting.
Kidneys damaged badly enough that he was passing blood during a race and kept going.
A heart with a hole in it that eventually required open heart surgery.
A body that, by his own description, is a ledger of surgeries and lasting injuries.
These are not badges.
They are wounds.

When he ran one hundred miles with no distance base and shattered his feet and hurt his kidneys, that was not a repeatable training method, it was a man doing damage to himself, and he would be among the first to tell you not to copy the exact dosage.
He injured himself repeatedly.
The story is inspiring, but the medical bill underneath it is very real.
And there is a deeper honesty in the book that is easy to miss under all the intensity.
Goggins does not claim this path made him happy.
He is candid that he is not a happy man in the ordinary sense of the word, not someone at peace, not someone content.
He drives himself out of a place that still contains the fear and the pain of that childhood, and the driving does not fully quiet it.
So if you are reading this book hoping it is a recipe for a joyful, balanced life, be clear-eyed: that is not what its author found or promises.
It is a philosophy of forging strength through suffering, and strength and happiness are not the same thing.
So how should you actually use this?
Here is the honest translation, the part I most want you to carry out of this episode.
The value of Goggins is not in the specific dosage.
It is in the principle.
You almost certainly do not need to run one hundred miles on broken feet.
You do not need to shatter yourself.
If you copy the dosage, you will get hurt, and you will probably miss the point.
What you should take instead is the single most useful idea in the whole book: your governor is engaging way too early.
The wall you hit in your workouts, in your work, in the hard conversation you keep avoiding, in the goal you talked yourself out of, that wall is almost certainly not your real limit.
It is a cushion that kicks in long before the edge.
And you can go find out, carefully and gradually, where your real limit actually is, by pushing a little past the false one, again and again, and building those calluses over time.
So scale it to your life.
Make the Accountability Mirror note tonight.
Start the Cookie Jar with the hard things you have already survived.

The next time your mind says you are done, treat it as forty percent and take a few more honest steps, then recover and rest like a sane person, because scaling the principle also means respecting your body in a way Goggins sometimes did not respect his.
Let discipline, not motivation, decide the cold mornings.
Rehearse the hard parts instead of the trophy.
And remember that the only opponent that finally matters is the voice you are arguing with when you want to quit.
And do not miss the quieter promise underneath all the noise, because it is the most hopeful thing in the entire book.
Goggins started from the bottom.
He was abused, he was hunted for the color of his skin, he could barely read, he failed the entrance exam twice, he quit once out of fear, and he spent years overweight and going nowhere.
None of that was chosen and none of it was fair.
And yet the thing that finally changed his life was not a gift from the outside.
No one handed him talent, or luck, or a rescue.
He changed by taking the one thing that was always his to control, his own effort and his own response to hardship, and refusing to give up on it.
That door is not reserved for extraordinary people.

It is standing right in front of you, in whatever ordinary hard thing you have been avoiding, and it opens the same way for everyone: not all at once, not dramatically, but through the plain, repeated, unglamorous act of doing the difficult thing one more time than you wanted to.
That is Can't Hurt Me by David Goggins.

A brutal, moving, and cautionary story about a man who was handed fear and pain as a child and spent his whole life turning them into fuel, who proved that the ceiling most of us accept is painted far too low, and who paid a real physical price to show us that our limits are usually further out than we think.
Take the principle.
Scale the dosage.
Go find out how much of yourself you have actually been using.
Thank you for listening to Listen Up, and I will see you on the next one.
